Data quality degraders: (1) Sparse profile attributes — if <30% of profiles have a critical feature (e.g., loyaltyTier is null for most profiles), the model cannot use it effectively; (2) Identity fragmentation — if many profiles have duplicate ECID-only fragments and the seed audience is disproportionately 'known' customers, the feature vectors are not comparable; (3) Seasonal bias in seed selection — building a lookalike from Black Friday purchasers produces a model biased toward holiday intent, not year-round purchasers; choose seed audiences from consistent, temporally stable behavior.

followups.q: How does 'Profile Enrichment via Audience Expansion' fit into a full-funnel marketing strategy on AEP?
followups.a: Full-funnel integration: (1) Top of funnel — use Audience Expansion (lookalike at factor 6-7) against a broad awareness segment to scale reach on programmatic channels (DV360, TTD) while maintaining behavioral relevance; (2) Mid-funnel — retargeting: use Audience Composition to intersect the Audience Expansion output with 'visited product page but no purchase' behavior, producing a high-intent retargeting audience; (3) Bottom of funnel — conversion: use the original seed audience directly for high-bid, narrow-targeting conversion campaigns; (4) Retention — use Audience Expansion against a 'churned customer' seed to find similar at-risk customers before they churn, feeding a Journey Optimizer proactive retention journey. Each funnel stage uses a different AEP capability — lookalike at top, Composition + streaming segmentation at mid, direct segment activation at bottom — demonstrating AEP as a full-funnel orchestration platform, not just a data store.

q: What is 'profile completeness' in AEP and how do you design an ingestion architecture that maximizes the percentage of profiles with high attribute coverage for segmentation?
a: Profile completeness refers to the percentage of fields in the union schema that have non-null values across your profile population — a highly complete profile has few null attributes, enabling richer segmentation conditions. Low completeness means segment rules on those attributes return zero results or under-sized audiences. Root causes of low completeness: (1) Source system data quality — CRM systems with optional fields not mandated for data entry (e.g., loyalty tier null for 40% of records because legacy onboarding didn't collect it); (2) Schema-dataset mismatch — a field is in the union schema from one dataset but that dataset only covers 20% of the addressable population; (3) Identity stitching failures — profile fragments from different systems are not linked, so a profile from the CRM has `loyaltyTier` populated but the Profile keyed on ECID (anonymous browser) has no CRM fragment attached. Architecture to maximize completeness: (1) Define a 'profile completeness score' computed attribute (`_tenantId.profileCompleteness`) that counts non-null values across 10 critical fields, updated daily; (2) Use this score in data quality dashboards and as a segmentation filter to exclude low-completeness profiles from precision campaigns; (3) Design identity-first ingestion — ensure every customer-facing touchpoint sends both ECID and a deterministic ID in every event so stitching fires as early as possible; (4) Prioritize high-completeness source systems (loyalty app with mandatory fields) as higher-ranked datasets in the merge policy so their attribute values win when conflict arises with sparser CRM data.
fj: A field being present in any XDM schema in the sandbox means all profiles will have a value for that field.
fs: A field appears in the union schema if any Profile-enabled dataset's schema contains it, but profiles only have a value for that field if a fragment from a dataset containing that field has been ingested for that specific customer identity — sparse source coverage directly causes null values at the profile level.

followups.q: How would you build a 'data quality scorecard' for AEP that tracks field completeness, identity resolution rate, and segment match rate weekly?
followups.a: Build three Query Service scheduled CTAS jobs (nightly): (1) Field completeness: `SELECT fieldName, COUNT(CASE WHEN fieldValue IS NOT NULL THEN 1 END) * 100.0 / COUNT(*) AS pct_complete FROM (SELECT EXPLODE(MAP_FROM_ENTRIES(ARRAYS_ZIP(ARRAY('loyaltyTier','email','phoneNumber'), ARRAY(loyaltyTier, email, phoneNumber)))) FROM profile_snapshot) GROUP BY fieldName` — produces a completeness % per field per day. (2) Identity resolution rate: `SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT crmId) AS known_profiles, COUNT(DISTINCT ecid) AS total_ecids, COUNT(DISTINCT crmId) * 100.0 / COUNT(DISTINCT ecid) AS resolution_rate FROM profile_snapshot` — tracks % of device profiles linked to a known person identity. (3) Segment match rate (for email sends): compare AEP exported audience size to ESP delivery acceptance rate — a >10% rejection rate indicates email identity quality issues (invalid addresses, old hashed emails). Write all three results to an AEP dataset with a weekly timestamp and visualize in Tableau via Query Service JDBC.

followups.q: What is 'segment suppression' and how do you implement a global do-not-contact list in AEP across all journeys?
followups.a: Segment suppression prevents a specific audience from receiving communications in a journey or activation. Implement a global do-not-contact (DNC) list in AEP: (1) Ingest the DNC list as a Profile-enabled dataset with a `_tenantId.doNotContact: true` boolean field and the CRM_ID as primary identity; (2) Create an AEP segment 'DNC List' on `doNotContact = true`; (3) In every Journey Optimizer journey, add an 'Exclude' audience block at the journey entry gate for the DNC List segment; (4) For batch destinations, use Audience Composition to exclude the DNC List from every audience before activation (Composition block: base audience EXCLUDE DNC List). For real-time consent, use the XDM Consent schema's `consents.marketing.any.val = 'n'` field as an AJO consent policy — this is evaluated per-profile at journey execution time and is the more robust mechanism for ongoing consent management. The DNC dataset approach is for external suppression lists (legal holds, litigation holds) not covered by standard consent fields.

followups.q: How does the AEP Platform Privacy Service handle a deletion request for a customer who has data spread across AEP, Adobe Analytics, Adobe Target, and Marketo?
followups.a: Adobe Privacy Service (POST /jobs with action: DELETE) orchestrates deletion across all Adobe product instances connected to the same Adobe Org. The job submits deletion requests in parallel to: (1) AEP Profile store (removes all profile fragments matching the submitted identity values across all datasets); (2) AEP Data Lake (issues Dataset Row Delete jobs via Data Hygiene for all datasets containing the identity — this runs asynchronously and may take up to 30 days); (3) AEP Identity Service (removes identity graph nodes and edges for the deleted identities); (4) Adobe Analytics (suppresses the ECID from report suite data via the Analytics Data Privacy API); (5) Adobe Target (removes visitor profile from Target's profile store); (6) Marketo Engage (triggers Marketo's GDPR delete workflow via the Marketo Privacy API integration). The Privacy Service job status dashboard tracks completion per product. Note: Analytics data deletion from historical report suites is not instant — Adobe Analytics suppresses future attribution but historical event data may remain in Adobe-managed infrastructure for up to 60 days before full deletion per Analytics' data lifecycle.

q: How does AEP's Streaming Ingestion HTTP API handle back-pressure, and what is the correct retry strategy for a mobile SDK that generates purchase events at high volume?
a: AEP's Streaming Ingestion endpoint returns HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) with a `Retry-After` header when the per-dataflow rate limit (1,000 requests/second) is exceeded. It returns HTTP 503 (Service Unavailable) during transient infrastructure issues. Correct retry strategy: implement exponential backoff with jitter — on 429, wait `Retry-After` seconds (usually 1-5s); on 503, start at 1s base delay, double each retry, add random jitter (±20%), cap at 60s, max 5 retries. Do NOT use linear retry (floods the endpoint on recovery) and do NOT drop events silently on 429 (causes data loss). For mobile SDKs: buffer events locally in a persistent queue (SQLite on-device) and process the queue on a background thread with back-pressure awareness. For purchase events specifically, treat them as high-priority (flush the queue immediately after purchase rather than waiting for the next batch flush cycle). Batch requests using the multi-event payload format (`Content-Type: application/json` array body, max 1,000 events or 1 MB) to reduce per-event request overhead — this converts 1,000 individual purchase events/sec into a single batched request, dramatically reducing 429 frequency.
fj: On a 429 response, immediately retry the same request in a tight loop until it succeeds.
fs: Tight retry loops on 429 amplify load on an already-overloaded endpoint, making recovery slower; use exponential backoff with jitter and a persistent local buffer to avoid data loss during sustained rate-limit periods.

followups.q: What is the difference between the AEP Streaming Validation Service and the XDM Schema validation that happens during batch ingestion?
followups.a: Streaming Validation Service performs a lightweight synchronous check on the incoming JSON payload envelope (required fields: `header`, `body`, and `_id` presence) and returns HTTP 400 for malformed JSON or missing envelope fields. Full XDM field-level validation (type checking, required sub-fields, enum constraints) is asynchronous — performed after the event is accepted into the Kafka buffer and before writing to the Profile store. Batch ingestion performs full XDM validation synchronously before writing any rows, using a Spark-based validator. The implication: a streaming event that passes the HTTP 200/400 gate can still fail XDM validation and end up in the dead-letter queue minutes later — always monitor the Data Monitoring UI error counts for streaming datasets, not just HTTP response codes.

followups.q: How do you implement a streaming data pipeline from a Kafka cluster to AEP without using the HTTP API?
followups.a: Use the AEP Apache Kafka Source Connector (a Kafka Connect sink connector). Install it in your Kafka Connect cluster, configure it with your AEP streaming endpoint URL and IMS service account credentials, and map Kafka topic-to-dataset routing. The connector reads from one or more Kafka topics, serializes messages to XDM JSON format (requires either pre-formatted XDM messages on the topic or a transformation using Kafka Connect's Single Message Transforms), and posts them to the AEP streaming endpoint. Throughput is much higher than direct HTTP API calls because the connector internally batches micro-batches and uses persistent connections. The connector is open-source (Adobe-maintained on GitHub at adobe/experience-platform-streaming-connect).

followups.q: What is Adobe I/O Events in the context of AEP and how do you use it to react to segment qualification events?
followups.a: Adobe I/O Events is Adobe's event routing service that publishes AEP platform events (segment qualification, profile ingestion, data flow run completion) to registered webhook endpoints or Adobe I/O Runtime serverless functions. To react to segment qualification: (1) Register an I/O Events integration in the Adobe Developer Console, subscribe to 'Real-Time Customer Profile' events, filter by `segmentMembership.change`; (2) Configure the Destinations connector 'Adobe I/O Events' as a streaming destination in AEP, mapping which segments' qualification events to publish; (3) Your registered webhook receives JSON payloads each time a profile enters or exits the segment. This enables non-AEP systems (custom apps, Slack bots, external CRMs) to react to audience qualification without polling the Profile API.

q: Explain the PQL syntax for a sequence-based segment ('customer who viewed a product page AND then added it to cart, all within 30 minutes') and why this fails streaming evaluation.
a: PQL sequence operator syntax: `select Profile from union where xEvent.eventType = 'productView' then [within 30 minutes] xEvent.eventType = 'commerce.productListAdds'`. The `then` operator enforces temporal ordering — event A must precede event B — and the `within` clause bounds the time gap. This is a valid PQL expression in the Segment Definition API. Why it fails streaming evaluation: AEP's streaming segmentation engine processes one event at a time as it arrives. When a `productView` event arrives, the engine has no way to know if a matching `commerce.productListAdds` will follow within 30 minutes without storing state for every in-progress sequence — maintaining per-profile sequence state at millions of events/second is not architecturally feasible in the streaming evaluation tier. Sequence operators (`then`) are therefore streaming-ineligible; AEP automatically downgrades this segment to batch evaluation (24-hour latency). Workaround for near-real-time behavior: use a computed attribute `addedToCartWithin30MinOfView` (updated hourly by a Query Service window function) and build a streaming-eligible simple attribute segment on that computed attribute's value.
fj: PQL sequence queries with 'then' operators are supported in streaming segmentation — they just have slightly higher latency.
fs: PQL `then` (sequence) operators are explicitly ineligible for streaming evaluation in AEP; segments using them always fall back to batch (24-hour) evaluation regardless of how simple the rest of the rule is.

followups.q: What PQL operators ARE supported in streaming segmentation (as of 2025)?
followups.a: Streaming-eligible PQL operators include: simple event existence checks (`xEvent.eventType = 'purchase'`), profile attribute comparisons (`loyaltyTier = 'Gold'`), boolean AND/OR/NOT between event and attribute conditions, `within last 24 hours` time-relative operator on event conditions (extended to 7 days for some event types as of 2025 changes), `inSegment()` only when the referenced segment is also streaming-eligible, and `count()` over events within a 24-hour window. Not supported: `then` (sequence), multi-entity joins (lookup dataset references), `inSegment()` against batch-only segments, aggregations over windows longer than 7 days, and funnel-step operators.

followups.q: How does AEP Segment Builder surface the 'streaming eligibility' indicator for a segment in progress?
followups.a: As you build a segment rule in the Segment Builder UI, the right panel shows the 'Evaluation method' badge which dynamically updates: 'Edge', 'Streaming', or 'Batch' — or 'Not eligible for streaming/edge' — as you add each rule block. The badge is computed in real-time by the Segment Evaluation Eligibility Service. If you add a `then` sequence operator or a multi-entity lookup, the badge immediately switches to 'Batch'. This UI feedback loop is critical — engineers should check this badge before finalizing a segment intended for real-time activation and refactor the rule if the badge shows 'Batch'.

followups.q: How would you approximate a session-based sequence segment in AEP without using the `then` operator?
followups.a: Decompose the sequence into a computed attribute: use a Query Service scheduled query to compute a boolean `hadCartAddWithinSessionOfProductView` per customer, where the logic is: `EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM events e1 JOIN events e2 ON e1.sessionId = e2.sessionId WHERE e1.eventType = 'productView' AND e2.eventType = 'commerce.productListAdds' AND e2.timestamp BETWEEN e1.timestamp AND e1.timestamp + INTERVAL '30 MINUTES' AND e1.identityId = e2.identityId)`. Write this computed attribute back to the Profile store (Profile-enabled dataset). Build a streaming-eligible segment on `hadCartAddWithinSessionOfProductView = true`. Latency: the computed attribute refreshes hourly, so activation lag is up to 1 hour — much better than 24-hour batch but not truly real-time. For sub-hour latency, use Adobe Analytics' session stitching on the data layer to fire a single composite event ('productViewThenAddToCart') and segment on that single event type, which IS streaming-eligible.

q: What is the AEP Data Hygiene feature set (Data Lifecycle Management), and how does it differ from Privacy Service deletions?
a: AEP Data Hygiene (rebranded Data Lifecycle Management in 2024) provides platform-native data expiry and deletion capabilities separate from the privacy-compliance-driven Privacy Service: (1) Dataset Expiry — set a time-to-live on an entire dataset; after the TTL, AEP automatically deletes the dataset and all its data lake records, Profile store fragments, and removes it from the Identity Graph. Configured via the Data Hygiene UI or POST /hygiene/ttl. (2) Record Delete (formerly 'Data Deletion Requests') — delete specific rows from a dataset identified by a field value (e.g., delete all rows where `crmId = '12345'`). Processes within 30 days. Configured via POST /hygiene/deleteRecord. (3) Dataset TTL for Profile — separately configure how long profile fragments from a dataset persist in the Profile store (separate from data lake retention). Privacy Service deletions, by contrast, are identity-centric (delete a PERSON across all systems), triggered by consumer rights requests (GDPR, CCPA), orchestrated across all Adobe products, and include audit trail generation for compliance evidence. Data Hygiene is for operational data management (cost control, retention policy enforcement); Privacy Service is for regulatory compliance. A common mistake is using Privacy Service for routine data cleanup (expensive, creates audit overhead) when Data Hygiene's Dataset Expiry is the appropriate tool.
fj: Privacy Service and Data Hygiene are the same thing — both delete data based on regulatory requirements.
fs: Data Hygiene is for operational data lifecycle management (retention TTLs, bulk record cleanup for cost or policy reasons); Privacy Service is for regulatory rights-based consumer deletion requests with cross-product orchestration and compliance audit trails — they serve different purposes and should not be substituted.

followups.q: What is the difference between 'Dataset Expiry' and 'Record Delete' in AEP Data Hygiene, and when would you use each?
followups.a: Dataset Expiry deletes the ENTIRE dataset (all rows, all batches) at a configured calendar date — used for temporary datasets (QA data, dev test data, time-boxed campaign data) that have a known end-of-life date. Record Delete removes specific rows from a dataset identified by a primary key value — used for targeted cleanup (e.g., delete all rows for a specific test account ID from a production dataset, or delete all events from a date range that was corrupted). Record Delete is more granular but slower (up to 30 days for full propagation through Profile store and data lake); Dataset Expiry is faster (the entire dataset is removed in one operation) but cannot be used when only a subset of records needs deletion.

followups.q: How does AEP Data Hygiene interact with the Identity Graph when deleting profile records?
followups.a: When a Record Delete request targets a CRM_ID, AEP's Data Hygiene service removes all dataset rows containing that identity value from the target dataset. It does NOT automatically remove the identity from the Identity Graph or delete profile fragments from other datasets — Data Hygiene is dataset-scoped, not identity-scoped. To fully remove a customer's identity from the Identity Graph, you need Privacy Service (which orchestrates identity graph node removal alongside profile store and data lake deletion). This distinction matters: a Data Hygiene Record Delete can orphan an identity in the graph (the graph has the node but no dataset rows back it up), causing phantom profile lookups that return empty fragments. Always use Privacy Service for full customer removal; use Data Hygiene only for dataset-level operational cleanup.

followups.q: How would you implement automatic data expiry for a sandbox-specific QA dataset that should delete all data 48 hours after each ingestion batch?
followups.a: AEP's Dataset Expiry feature sets a single calendar-date expiry for an entire dataset, not a rolling 48-hour TTL per batch. For per-batch TTL: (1) Create a Data Hygiene schedule using the Record Delete API (not Dataset Expiry) — set a scheduled job that runs every 24 hours, querying for rows with `ingestDate < CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - INTERVAL '48 HOURS'` and submitting delete requests for those rows' primary key values; (2) Alternatively, use a Query Service scheduled DELETE (not natively supported — Query Service is SELECT/CTAS only) workaround: CTAS the surviving rows (less than 48 hours old) to a new temp dataset, swap the dataset references, and expire the old dataset. For pure QA sandbox cleanup, the pragmatic approach is a weekly sandbox reset (wiping the entire dev sandbox via the Sandbox Reset API) rather than fine-grained per-batch expiry.

q: How does AEP's 'Computed Attributes' feature differ from a standard Query Service CTAS derived dataset, and when does a computed attribute propagate to the union schema?
a: Computed Attributes are a dedicated AEP service that defines profile-level scalar aggregations on top of ExperienceEvent history, with first-class integration into the Profile store and Segmentation Service. Key differences from CTAS: (1) Lifecycle management — Computed Attributes have a UI-managed definition, version history, and enable/disable toggle; CTAS is a raw SQL query with no built-in lifecycle management. (2) Profile store integration — Computed Attribute values are written directly as fields in the Profile store (under `_tenantId.computedAttributes`) on a configurable refresh schedule (daily or hourly); CTAS writes to a dataset that must be separately Profile-enabled and identity-mapped. (3) Streaming-eligible — a subset of Computed Attribute types (event count, event sum with time-window) support streaming evaluation, updating the attribute value within seconds of a qualifying event arrival; CTAS is batch-only. (4) Union schema propagation — Computed Attributes appear in the union schema automatically once enabled (no need to create a schema or field group separately); CTAS-to-Profile requires manual schema creation and Profile-enablement. When a Computed Attribute propagates: after the attribute is created and enabled, the definition creates a new field in the union schema within minutes. The first values appear after the first scheduled computation run (up to 24 hours for daily cadence). Profile lookups and Segment Builder will show the field as soon as it appears in the union schema, but values will be null until the first computation completes.
fj: Computed Attributes and CTAS-derived datasets are equivalent — both write to the Profile store in the same way.
fs: Computed Attributes have native union schema propagation, streaming evaluation support, and managed lifecycle; CTAS requires manual schema creation, dataset Profile-enablement, and identity mapping, and is always batch-evaluated — they are not interchangeable for real-time use cases.

followups.q: What types of aggregation functions are supported for streaming Computed Attributes in AEP?
followups.a: As of 2025, streaming-eligible Computed Attribute aggregation types are: `count` (count of events matching a filter within a time window), `sum` (sum of a numeric field across matching events in a time window), `min`, `max`, and `mostRecentValue` (the value of a field from the most recent matching event). Time windows for streaming: 24 hours, 7 days, 30 days, 60 days, 90 days. NOT streaming-eligible: `average`, `countDistinct`, `percentile`, and any aggregation requiring cross-profile joins. For complex aggregations not supported by Computed Attributes' streaming mode, use a daily-refresh Computed Attribute (falls back to batch computation) or a Query Service CTAS running on a scheduled cadence.

followups.q: How do you handle a Computed Attribute that references a deprecated XDM field that was removed from the schema?
followups.a: XDM schemas in AEP are append-only — fields cannot be removed once data is ingested. So a 'removed' field scenario typically means: (a) the field is no longer populated in new ingestion but still exists in the schema (no structural removal); or (b) a new schema version was created with the field absent and data moved to the new dataset. For case (a), the Computed Attribute continues to aggregate over historical events that had the field, plus null values for new events — the aggregate will gradually reflect less data over time as the event TTL expires. For case (b), update the Computed Attribute definition to reference the new field path in the new schema and schema. If the Computed Attribute's XDM path no longer resolves to any field in the union schema (because the dataset was deprecated), the attribute will return null for all profiles — disable it and create a new one targeting the replacement schema.

followups.q: What is the 'lookback period' in a Computed Attribute definition and how does it interact with the profile's stored event history window?
followups.a: The lookback period in a Computed Attribute definition (e.g., 'sum of purchase amounts in the last 90 days') specifies the time window over which events are aggregated. AEP's Profile store retains ExperienceEvent history for the duration configured in the dataset's Data Expiry TTL. If the dataset TTL is 6 months (180 days) and your Computed Attribute lookback is 90 days, the computation scans 90 days of events — working correctly. If you set a 365-day lookback but the dataset TTL is 180 days, the attribute silently computes over only 180 days of available history — the lookback is bounded by the actual event retention, not the configured window. Always ensure dataset TTL >= the maximum Computed Attribute lookback period used in your segmentation use cases.

q: What does 'AEP profile fragment' mean precisely, and how does the Profile Access API assemble a unified view at query time?
a: A profile fragment is a single record stored in the AEP Profile store that corresponds to one row ingested into one Profile-enabled dataset for one primary identity value. A single real-world customer represented across 5 datasets (CRM, loyalty app, web events profile, email platform, POS) will have 5 separate profile fragments in the Profile store, all linked via the Identity Graph. Each fragment contains only the fields defined in its source dataset's XDM schema — a CRM fragment has `firstName`, `loyaltyTier`, `crmId`; a loyalty fragment has `pointsBalance`, `tierExpiryDate`, `loyaltyId`. When you call GET /access/entities with a customer's email address, the Profile Access API: (1) resolves the email to its identity cluster via the Identity Graph; (2) retrieves all profile fragments for all identity values in the cluster across all datasets; (3) applies the active merge policy to resolve field-level conflicts and select winning values for fields present in multiple fragments; (4) returns a single JSON object representing the unified, merged profile. The union schema defines all possible fields in this JSON response. Latency of this assembly: 50–200ms typical for a well-stitched cluster of 2–5 fragments; 200–500ms+ for wide clusters with 20+ fragments due to Cassandra multi-read fan-out. Profile retrieval is NOT real-time in the database-row-update sense — it is dynamic assembly every time, which is why high-throughput real-time serving needs the edge projection cache rather than direct Profile API calls.
fj: The Profile Access API reads from a single, continuously updated master record for each customer — there are no fragments, it's just one row per customer.
fs: The Profile store contains multiple immutable fragments (one per source dataset per identity); the unified profile is dynamically assembled at query time by applying a merge policy to all fragments in the identity cluster — there is no pre-materialized master row.

followups.q: What is the Profile Store's write path when a new streaming event arrives, and how quickly is it reflected in a profile lookup?
followups.a: When a streaming event arrives via the HTTP API source: (1) The event is accepted to the Kafka ingestion buffer (synchronous, <100ms); (2) The ingestion pipeline validates XDM and writes the event row to the data lake's staging area as Parquet (asynchronous, 1–5 seconds); (3) For Profile-enabled datasets, the ingestion pipeline simultaneously writes/updates the profile fragment to the Cassandra-backed Profile store key-value layer (1–3 seconds); (4) The Identity Service graph is updated if the event contains new identity links (1–3 seconds). After step (3), the profile is available via GET /access/entities with the updated fragment. Total end-to-end from HTTP POST to profile lookup availability: typically 2–5 seconds at p95 under normal load. For streaming segmentation: the segment membership evaluation runs after Identity Service graph update, typically adding 2–5 additional seconds — total qualification latency 5–10 seconds.

followups.q: How do you retrieve all profile fragments for a customer (not just the merged view) via the Profile API?
followups.a: Use the Profile Fragments API: GET /access/entities?schema.name=_xdm.context.profile&entityId=<id>&entityIdNS=<namespace>&fields=identityMap and then GET /access/entities with the `fields` parameter set to `*` to retrieve the full merged view. To get unmerged fragments per dataset, use the Segmentation Service export API or Query Service: `SELECT * FROM <profile_dataset_name> WHERE identityMap['CRM_ID'][0].id = '<crmId>'` — each row in the dataset IS a profile fragment. For operational debugging, the Profile Fragments API endpoint GET /access/entities with `?sourceName=<datasetId>` parameter filters fragments to a specific source dataset, allowing you to inspect exactly what each source contributed before merge.

followups.q: What is the 'Profile Preview' feature in AEP Segment Builder and how accurate is it?
followups.a: Profile Preview in Segment Builder (the 'Estimated profiles' count and the sample profiles list) uses a 10% statistical sample of the profile snapshot to estimate segment size and show representative profiles. It runs automatically after a debounce delay as you build rules. Accuracy: ±15% for segments >100K profiles (reasonable sample size); ±50% or worse for small segments (<10K profiles) because the 10% sample may contain too few matching profiles for reliable extrapolation. The sample is also bounded by the last daily Profile Snapshot — it reflects yesterday's profile state, not today's real-time state. Do not use the Preview count for campaign budgeting decisions; always run a full Segment Job (POST /segment/jobs) for the official member count before activating to paid media destinations with CPC-based budgeting.

q: How does AEP's 'multi-entity segmentation' work for a use case like 'customers who purchased a product currently on recall' — describe the schema design, relationship descriptor, and PQL syntax?
a: Multi-entity segmentation lets PQL join profile/event data against a lookup (non-profile) dataset — for example, a product recall registry. Schema design: (1) ExperienceEvent schema with a `commerce.productListItems[].SKU` field (standard XDM Commerce field group); (2) Product Recall lookup schema — a custom XDM Individual Profile or custom class schema with fields `recalledSKU` (string, required, marked as primary identity with a custom 'RECALLED_SKU' namespace), `recallDate`, `recallReason`. This is NOT Profile-enabled — it is a lookup dataset. Relationship descriptor: register a xdm:descriptorOneToOne (schema-to-schema relationship) in the Schema Registry pointing from `ExperienceEvent.commerce.productListItems.SKU` to the Recall schema's `recalledSKU` field via POST /descriptors with type `xdm:descriptorRelationship`. Once the relationship is defined, the Segment Builder 'XDM ExperienceEvent' block shows a relationship path 'Related Recall Record' available for conditions. PQL syntax: `select Profile from union where xEvent.eventType = 'commerce.purchases' and xEvent.commerce.productListItems.exists(p, p._relatedRecall.recallDate >= '2025-01-01')`. The `_relatedRecall` accessor is the relationship traversal operator. Limitation: multi-entity segments using lookup datasets are always batch-evaluated — the lookup join is a Spark SQL join against a Parquet dataset, not feasible in streaming mode.
fj: You can look up a product catalog dataset in a streaming PQL segment by adding it as a secondary XDM field in the profile schema.
fs: Lookup dataset joins in PQL require a schema relationship descriptor and are only evaluated in batch mode; streaming segmentation cannot perform lookup joins because it processes single events without a distributed dataset join at evaluation time.

followups.q: How do you update a lookup dataset used in a multi-entity segment in real time — for example, adding a new recalled SKU that should immediately affect segment membership?
followups.a: Lookup datasets in multi-entity segmentation are batch-refreshed — you ingest a new record to the lookup dataset (via Batch Ingestion or a Sources connector), and the new SKU appears in the lookup table. Segment membership updates happen on the next batch segmentation run (up to 24 hours). There is no streaming path for lookup dataset updates in multi-entity segments. For the product recall use case, this 24-hour lag is unacceptable for safety-critical notifications. Workaround: for the immediate notification use case, bypass segmentation entirely — build the affected customer list with a Query Service CTAS (joining event history against the recall lookup table directly), upload as an External Audience, and trigger the Journey Optimizer journey immediately (as described in the recall incident design pattern). Reserve the multi-entity segment for downstream reporting and campaign suppression, not the initial emergency notification.

followups.q: What is the maximum number of relationship descriptors (multi-entity lookups) you can chain in a single PQL query?
followups.a: AEP supports a maximum of 2 levels of relationship traversal in a single PQL segment definition (e.g., Event → Product → ProductCategory is allowed; Event → Product → ProductCategory → Supplier is not). Beyond 2 levels, the PQL parser returns a validation error. This constraint exists because each relationship traversal adds a Spark join to the batch evaluation job — deep join chains cause exponential data expansion and query timeouts. For use cases requiring deeper lookups, pre-materialize the joined data in Query Service as a denormalized lookup dataset and use a single-level relationship to that dataset.

followups.q: How does AEP handle updates to a lookup dataset when a relationship-dependent segment has already been activated to a destination?
followups.a: When the lookup dataset receives a new batch (e.g., a new recalled SKU is added), the next scheduled batch segmentation run re-evaluates the multi-entity segment against the updated lookup. Profiles that previously did not qualify (purchased the SKU before it was added to the recall list) will newly enter the segment. The destination activation job will detect these new entrants and send 'segment entered' events to the destination (streaming destination) or include them in the next full/incremental export (batch destination). There is no retroactive notification for the time between the recall being added to the lookup and the next batch segmentation run — this is the inherent latency limitation that makes Query Service CTAS + manual audience upload the preferred path for time-critical lookup-dependent audience updates.

q: As a Principal Engineer, how would you evaluate whether to build a real-time personalization layer on top of AEP vs. migrating to a purpose-built CDP (Segment, mParticle) — what framework drives the decision?
a: Decision framework across six dimensions: (1) Ecosystem integration depth — if the business already uses Adobe Analytics, Adobe Target, Adobe Campaign, and Marketo, AEP's native connectors eliminate weeks of custom integration work per service; a purpose-built CDP (Segment/Twilio) requires building each integration individually. Count the existing Adobe ecosystem footprint: if >3 Adobe products are in production, AEP integration ROI is strong. (2) Identity model fit — AEP's deterministic identity graph and ECID-native architecture suits B2C businesses with first-party web data; Segment's spec-based model is more ETL-friendly for polyglot data stacks (non-Adobe sources). (3) Real-time latency SLAs — AEP streaming segmentation has 2–10s lag; mParticle's Audience Builder has comparable performance; none of these platforms achieve <100ms feature serving without edge caching (you need CDN-edge logic regardless). If your primary use case is sub-100ms decisioning, build a custom feature store with Redis regardless of CDP choice. (4) ML maturity — AEP DSW + Customer AI is a managed ML stack that needs minimal data science ops overhead; if your team has strong MLOps and uses SageMaker or Vertex AI, piping those model outputs into any CDP via batch ingestion is equivalent in capability. (5) Governance and compliance overhead — AEP's DULE + Privacy Service + Consent framework is enterprise-grade out-of-box; alternatives require significant custom governance engineering. (6) Cost at scale — AEP licensing (profile-based) becomes expensive >50M addressable profiles; Segment/mParticle have event-based pricing that may be cheaper for high-event, lower-profile-count workloads. Decision rule: if ≥3 Adobe products in production AND governance requirements are complex AND team lacks MLOps depth → AEP. If mostly non-Adobe stack AND ML is custom AND profile count <20M → Segment/mParticle. Mixed: hybrid architecture (AEP for Adobe activation layer, Segment for event streaming and non-Adobe integrations).
fj: Choosing a CDP is primarily a features comparison — whichever platform has more connectors is the right choice.
fs: CDP platform selection is primarily an ecosystem fit, team capability, governance requirements, and total cost of ownership decision — connector count is a secondary concern easily overridden by deep ecosystem coupling, regulatory compliance needs, and the operational cost of managing custom integrations.

followups.q: What is the 'AEP as data mesh hub' architectural pattern and how does it contrast with using AEP as a centralized CDP?
followups.a: In the centralized CDP pattern, AEP is the system of record for all customer data — every source lands in AEP, and all activation flows out of AEP. In the data mesh hub pattern, AEP is one domain among many: the marketing domain owns AEP as its activation layer, but the data science domain owns a Databricks lakehouse, the operational domain owns Salesforce, and a data mesh governance layer (Apache Atlas, Collibra) manages cross-domain schema contracts. AEP in this pattern consumes data from the data mesh via Federated Audience Composition or targeted batch ingestion, rather than ingesting everything. This reduces AEP storage costs, preserves data sovereignty within source domains, and avoids making AEP a single point of failure for non-marketing use cases — at the cost of more complex data lineage and identity resolution that spans multiple systems.

followups.q: How do you build a business case for AEP licensing cost to a CFO skeptical of the price premium over open-source alternatives?
followups.a: Quantify three ROI components: (1) Engineering cost avoidance — estimate the cost of building equivalent capabilities (identity graph: 6–12 months of 2 senior engineers; DULE governance: 3–6 months; Privacy Service automation: 2–3 months; 100+ pre-built connectors: ongoing integration maintenance). At $200K fully-loaded senior engineer cost, avoiding 18 months of 3 engineers = $900K saved. (2) Time to value — AEP cuts deployment timeline from 24 months (custom build) to 6–9 months, enabling 15 months of earlier revenue from personalization and retention campaigns. At even 0.5% revenue lift on $500M revenue = $2.5M annual incremental, 15 months earlier = $3.1M NPV. (3) Risk reduction — Privacy Service automation reduces GDPR violation risk (fines up to 4% of annual global turnover) and SOC 2 audit cost reduction from pre-built Audit Trail. Present the full 3-year TCO comparison: AEP licensing vs (open-source infrastructure + engineering headcount + compliance risk). For enterprises above $100M revenue with complex governance, AEP typically shows positive ROI within 18 months.

followups.q: How would you design a multi-region AEP deployment for a global company that must keep EU customer data in the EU and US data in the US?
followups.a: AEP's data residency is configured at the org level — Adobe offers EU-hosted AEP orgs (data center in the Netherlands/Ireland) and US-hosted orgs (Oregon/Virginia). A multi-region architecture: (1) Provision two AEP orgs: one EU org and one US org — not sandboxes within one org, because sandboxes share the underlying infrastructure and data residency is org-level. (2) Geo-routing at the collection layer: Adobe Edge Network automatically routes data to the nearest PoP, but you must configure the `imsOrgId` in alloy.js to point to the correct org based on customer geography — detect customer location via server-side geo-IP and inject the correct AEP streaming endpoint. (3) Cross-region customer recognition: for customers who travel (a US customer visiting the EU site), you need a shared identity resolution layer external to AEP (a master ID resolution service) that maps identities between the two orgs — AEP orgs do not share identity graphs. (4) Governance: DULE labels and consent configurations must be duplicated in both orgs using Sandbox Tooling-equivalent org-level export/import scripts. This architecture is complex and expensive (two full AEP contracts) — evaluate whether regional data controllers can be structured to allow the EU entity to process data on the US org with Standard Contractual Clauses, which is often the pragmatic path.

q: Explain the AEP Real-Time CDP B2B Edition data model. What are Account Profiles, Buying Groups, and Opportunities, and how does B2B segmentation differ from standard B2C segmentation?
a: B2B Edition introduces four first-class XDM entities that do not exist in standard CDP: Account (maps to XDM Business Account class), Opportunity (XDM Business Opportunity), Campaign Member (XDM Business Campaign Member), and Marketing List Member. Account Profiles are the B2B analog of Individual Profiles — a unified, identity-resolved record representing a company, not a person, assembled from CRM account records and website firmographic enrichment. Buying Groups link a set of known Individual Profiles (e.g., the VP of Engineering, Procurement Lead, and CFO at a target account) to a specific Product Interest, capturing the reality that enterprise deals involve multiple stakeholders. Segmentation differences: (1) Standard CDP segments evaluate predicates against Individual Profile attributes and ExperienceEvent history. B2B Edition adds a third evaluation context — Account Profiles — enabling account-based segments ('all accounts in Healthcare vertical with >$500M revenue and at least 2 open opportunities past 90 days'). (2) B2B segments can use multi-entity lookups across the Account→Opportunity→Person relationship graph, e.g., 'people at accounts where any linked opportunity is in Proposal stage'. (3) Buying Group completeness scoring ('buying group role assignment >= 80%') can gate journey entry, replacing the individual propensity models used in B2C. Activation is ABM-shaped: B2B destinations (LinkedIn Matched Audiences, 6sense) accept account lists, so AEP B2B Edition emits account-level segments with constituent person identities attached, not just individual-level hashed email audiences.
fj: B2B Edition segmentation works the same as B2C — you build segments against Individual Profiles and just add firmographic attributes from the account record.
fs: B2B Edition introduces a separate Account Profile store with its own union schema; segments can be evaluated at the account level, person level, or across the Account→Opportunity→Person relationship graph, which requires a fundamentally different data model and activation path compared to B2C person-centric segmentation.

followups.q: How does AEP B2B Edition resolve the identity between an Account Profile and its constituent Individual Profiles, and what is the account-to-person relationship object?
followups.a: The linkage is stored in the XDM Business Account Person Relation schema. Each row represents a many-to-many edge: one Account ID (B2B source key) to one Person ID (B2B source key), with a `personRole` field (e.g., 'Decision Maker', 'Technical Evaluator'). AEP's Profile merge for B2B ingests these relation records and builds a traversable graph: from any Individual Profile, you can resolve which Account Profiles they belong to, and from any Account Profile, you can enumerate the constituent person identities. This relation schema must be ingested and Profile-enabled alongside the Account and Person schemas for the multi-entity segmentation queries to work — omitting it means B2B segments that join across account and person will return zero results even if both entity types have data.

followups.q: What is Buying Group completeness score and how is it computed in AEP B2B Edition?
followups.a: Completeness score is a system-computed percentage (0–100) stored on the Buying Group record that measures how many of the expected role slots for a product interest have been filled with known, identified individuals. A 'buying committee template' defines the required roles (e.g., Economic Buyer, Champion, Technical Reviewer, Legal Approver). As Account→Person relations are ingested and matched to product interests, AEP increments the completeness score. An Engagement Score is also maintained — a weighted sum of ExperienceEvent interactions (email opens, web visits, webinar attendance) from the persons in the buying group. Journey Optimizer B2B Edition uses both scores as journey entry/branch conditions: e.g., only enter an account into a high-touch sales sequence when completeness >= 60% AND engagement score >= 50, indicating a real committee is forming.

followups.q: What are the CRM connector requirements for ingesting Salesforce data into AEP B2B Edition, and what is the key schema mapping difference for Opportunities vs. standard B2C objects?
followups.a: The AEP Salesforce CRM source connector in B2B mode ingests Account, Contact, Lead, Opportunity, Campaign, and CampaignMember objects via the Salesforce Bulk API 2.0. Key difference: in B2B mode, the connector maps Salesforce Opportunity to the XDM Business Opportunity class (not to ExperienceEvents). This means Opportunity records are stored as record-type profile data with their own identity namespace ('salesforceOpportunityId'), not as time-series events. Consequently, Opportunity attributes (stage, amount, close date) are queryable in segments as record attributes, not event filters. The critical mapping requirement: Salesforce AccountId on the Opportunity record must be mapped to the B2B Account source key so AEP can link the Opportunity to the Account Profile. Without this mapping, Opportunities are ingested but exist as orphaned records with no account context, and account-level multi-entity segments that filter on opportunity stage will return empty results.

q: A production AEP sandbox has identity graph fragmentation: customers who should be stitched into a single profile exist as two or more separate Profile IDs. Walk through your full diagnostic and remediation process.
a: Fragmentation occurs when the identity graph cannot find a shared identity namespace node to stitch two or more profile fragments together. Step 1 — Confirm fragmentation. Query the Profile API: `GET /access/entities?schema.name=_xdm.context.profile&entityId=<email>&entityIdNS=Email` returns all profile fragments associated with that email. If it returns multiple `recordId` values instead of one merged profile, fragmentation is confirmed. Also run Query Service: `SELECT identityMap, _id, COUNT(*) FROM profile_snapshot_export WHERE email = 'user@example.com' GROUP BY identityMap, _id` to see whether the ECID, CRM ID, and Email identity are appearing in separate rows. Step 2 — Identify the broken link. Use the Identity Service Graph Viewer (UI) or `GET /identity/cluster/members?xid=<encoded-identity>` to retrieve the full identity cluster for the suspected profile. Look for which identity namespace is absent from the cluster that should be present — typically CRM ID is missing in one fragment (a web-only visitor) and Email is missing in another (a CRM record with no web events). Step 3 — Trace ingestion root cause. Pull the batch ingestion history for datasets mapped to both fragments. Common causes: (a) the CRM dataset maps CRMID as identity but the email field is NOT mapped as an identity descriptor — so email exists as a plain profile attribute, not as an identity node in the graph; (b) the identity namespace for the email is set to 'email' (lowercase custom namespace) in one source and 'Email' (standard namespace) in another — namespace matching is case-sensitive; (c) a CRMID value ingested as '00123' in one source and '123' in another (leading-zero formatting difference) creates two separate CRMID nodes that never stitch. Step 4 — Fix namespace/identity descriptor config. If the identity descriptor is missing, add it via `POST /schemaregistry/tenant/descriptors` with `@type: xdm:descriptorIdentity`, the correct namespace, and `xdm:isPrimary: false`. New ingestion will begin creating identity graph edges. Step 5 — Handle historical fragments. The identity graph only stitches newly ingested events forward; existing fragmented profile fragments are not retroactively merged until new data arrives that creates the missing link. Force a re-stitch by re-ingesting a 'bridge' batch: a CSV with CRMID + Email columns where both are mapped as identity descriptors, covering all fragmented CRM IDs. This synthetic batch creates the missing identity graph edges and triggers a profile merge for existing fragments. Step 6 — Validate. After re-ingestion, re-run the Graph Viewer query and confirm the previously separate nodes now appear in a single cluster. Check profile count in the Profile UI — correctly merged profiles reduce total Profile count.
fj: If two profiles share the same email address but the identity graph hasn't stitched them, re-ingesting the event data for both will automatically fix the fragmentation.
fs: Re-ingesting event data alone does not fix fragmentation if the root cause is a missing identity descriptor or namespace mismatch; you must first correct the descriptor configuration so the namespace is recognized as an identity node, then ingest a bridge batch that carries both identity values to create the missing graph edge.

followups.q: What is the identity graph shared device problem and how do AEP identity settings mitigate it?
followups.a: Shared device collapse occurs when multiple real people use the same device (e.g., a family tablet, a call-center PC), causing their ECIDs to be linked into a single identity cluster. In a household scenario, if Person A logs in on a tablet and generates purchase events, then Person B logs in on the same tablet, AEP links both CRM IDs to the same ECID, eventually collapsing them into one profile — producing nonsensical merge results (two people's behavioral history merged). Mitigation: (1) enable the Shared Device toggle on the ECID namespace in Identity Settings, which causes AEP to treat ECID as a 'non-priority' namespace — it still links identities but the identity graph algorithm uses CRM ID / Email as the authoritative 'unique' identifiers and does not allow a single ECID to be the bridge that merges two CRM IDs; (2) configure identity graph algorithm settings to set 'unique' flag on CRM ID namespace, limiting each CRM ID to appear in exactly one identity cluster regardless of shared device signals.

followups.q: How do you use the Identity Service's `/identity/cluster/history` endpoint to audit when a fragmentation event occurred?
followups.a: `GET /identity/cluster/history?xid=<encoded-identity>&graph-type=Private` returns a time-ordered list of cluster membership change events for a given identity — each entry shows a timestamp, the operation type (MERGE or SPLIT), and the set of XIDs that were added to or removed from the cluster. By querying this endpoint for one of the fragmented profile's known identities, you can pinpoint the exact timestamp when the CRM ID and Email node were part of the same cluster vs. when they split (or never joined). This is the definitive audit trail for root-causing whether fragmentation happened at initial ingestion (the link was never created) or was introduced by a subsequent data change (e.g., a CRM record update that wiped the email field, removing the identity value from the graph).

followups.q: What is the impact of identity graph fragmentation on Journey Optimizer journey execution, and how do you detect affected journeys?
followups.a: When a customer's profile is fragmented, Journey Optimizer evaluates journey entry eligibility and personalization lookups against whichever profile fragment matches the trigger event's identity. If the trigger event carries an ECID (web click) but the email preference and consent flags live in the CRM-sourced fragment (a different profile), the journey entry check may see incomplete consent data and suppress the journey, or the personalization in the first email step will return null values for CRM-sourced attributes. Detection approach: in Journey reports, filter for journeys with high 'profile not found' or 'personalization error' rates in the action logs (GET /journeyVersions/{id}/actionExecutions filtered by errorCode). Cross-reference the affected profile IDs against the identity graph cluster API to confirm they are the fragmented profiles. The remediation is the same bridge-batch re-stitch, but journeys that already suppressed or errored for affected profiles will not automatically re-execute — you must re-trigger entry for those profiles after the stitch is complete using a one-time re-entry audience activation.
